metadata.title: DWSIM Equipment Fixed Capital Cost (CAPEX) Dataset
metadata.purpose: Reference dataset + correlation constants for estimating purchased and installed (bare module) equipment cost for every physical unit operation available in DWSIM, for use in a free/open DWSIM-based CAPEX estimator.
metadata.method: Module Costing Technique (Turton et al., CAPCOST) — purchased cost as a function of a single capacity/size parameter A, escalated to an installed 'bare module' cost via material and pressure correction factors.

cepci_reference_values.note: CEPCI publication moved behind a paid subscription after August 2024; the 2024/2025 figures above are approximate annual averages reported in secondary sources, not the exact published index. Always re-check the current value before relying on an escalated cost — see README.md for where to look it up. Escalate cost with: Cost_new = Cost_2001 * (CEPCI_new / 394.3).

1_purchased_cost.description: Purchased equipment cost at ambient pressure and carbon-steel construction, Cp0, as a function of capacity/size parameter A.
1_purchased_cost.out_of_range_rule: If A falls outside [Amin, Amax], do NOT trust the polynomial directly. Evaluate Cp0 at the nearest bound (Amin or Amax), then scale exponentially: Cp0(A) = Cp0(Abound) * (A / Abound)^n, using the 'n' exponent given for that equipment type. Always log a warning when this happens — it means you are extrapolating outside the correlation's validated range.

2_bare_module_factor_standard.description: Applies to pumps, heat exchangers, vessels/towers and trays.
2_bare_module_factor_standard.equation: FBM = max(1, B1 + B2 * FM * FP)
2_bare_module_factor_standard.FM: Material factor (dimensionless, relative to carbon steel = 1.0; from material_factors table).
2_bare_module_factor_standard.FP: Pressure factor (dimensionless, =1 at/near ambient pressure; from pressure_factor_correlations, except vessels which use a dedicated thickness-based formula — see '3_vessel_pressure_factor').

3_vessel_pressure_factor.description: Vessels/towers do not use the C1/C2/C3 polynomial. FP is derived from the required shell thickness, which itself depends on design pressure and diameter.
3_vessel_pressure_factor.equation_vacuum: If design pressure Pd < -0.5 barg (vacuum service): FP = 1.25
3_vessel_pressure_factor.equation_thin_shell: If Pd >= -0.5 barg and computed shell thickness ts < 0.25 in (the universal minimum allowable thickness): FP = 1.0
3_vessel_pressure_factor.units: Pd in barg, D = internal diameter in metres. This reproduces the shell-thickness-based pressure factor used by the CAPCOST method; if you already compute mechanical wall thickness elsewhere in your sizing code, you can substitute that thickness directly.

4_ancillary_pressure_factor.description: Applies to pumps and heat exchangers (anything that is not a vessel).
4_ancillary_pressure_factor.units: P = gauge pressure in barg. Valid only within [Pmin, Pmax]; outside that range, clip to the nearest bound before evaluating (same extrapolation caution as the purchased-cost formula).

5_bare_module_cost.description: Installed cost of one piece of equipment, including direct materials (piping, concrete, steel, insulation) and labour for that item, but not contingency/fee or auxiliary/offsite facilities.

6_total_module_cost.description: Adds 18% for contractor's contingency and fee on top of the summed bare module costs of every piece of equipment in the flowsheet. Appropriate for a brownfield/retrofit project where general site facilities already exist.

7_grassroots_cost.description: Adds an allowance for auxiliary/offsite facilities (new roads, land, utility supply, administration buildings, etc.) for a brand-new ('grassroots') plant, sized as a fraction of the SUM of all purchased equipment costs at base (ambient/CS) conditions. NOTE: Turton's published constant is 0.35 in current editions (older sources circulate 0.50 — if your reference uses 0.50, that is also defensible, just be internally consistent and state which you used).

8_tray_quantity_factor.description: Distillation/absorption column trays are costed per tray (by tray area) and then multiplied by the number of trays AND a quantity discount factor Fq, since the K1/K2/K3 correlation already represents a 'per unit' cost that doesn't fall fast enough for very tall columns.
8_tray_quantity_factor.confidence: medium — double check the exact Fq constants (2.25, 1.0414) against your specific edition of Turton before relying on it for a large number of trays.

9_escalation.description: All K1/K2/K3 correlations below return cost in 2001 USD. Escalate to the present using the current CEPCI (see metadata.cepci_reference_values).

pump.notes: Driver (electric motor) cost is bundled into this correlation; do not cost a separate motor on top of the pump unless you are deliberately doing a detailed (not conceptual) estimate.

compressor.notes: Turton's compressor correlation already includes the driver in the purchased cost, and treats the bare module factor as a flat, pressure-independent lookup by material (NOT the generic B1+B2*FM*FP formula). Map DWSIM's Compressor object using 'Power Required' (kW) as A. DWSIM's Expander object is the reverse machine and is costed separately under 'turbine' below.

turbine_expander.capacity_parameter: Shaft power output, kW (DWSIM: Expander 'Power Generated/Required')

axial_gas_turbine_expander.confidence: medium — K1/K2/K3 corroborated against two independent secondary sources quoting Turton; the bare-module-factor-by-material figures are a literature-typical estimate scaled from the compressor pattern, not independently re-verified against the primary table. Amin/Amax/n are reasonable estimates, not independently confirmed — verify before using outside the 100-4000 kW range.

turbine_expander.notes: Used for DWSIM's 'Expander' unit operation (turbo-expander / gas or steam turbine). If your expander is a small reciprocating or screw-type unit instead, the compressor reciprocating/rotary correlation run 'in reverse' (still sized on kW) is a closer analogy.

heat_exchanger.capacity_parameter: Heat transfer area, m^2 (A = Q / (U * LMTD); see README for how to get A from a DWSIM Heater/Cooler that only reports duty Q).

material_factor_combinations.note: Shell/tube order does not matter for the factor value (CS/SS == SS/CS).

heat_exchanger.notes: DWSIM's 'Heater' and 'Cooler' objects only report a duty Q (kW) and outlet conditions, not a physical area — treat them as a single-stream-side heat exchanger against a utility (steam/hot oil for Heater, cooling water/refrigerant for Cooler), pick a representative overall U from a standard table (e.g. 500-1500 W/m2K for water-to-liquid duties, 30-300 for gas duties), compute LMTD from the known process-side T's and an assumed utility supply/return T, then A = Q/(U*LMTD). Use 'fixed_tube_sheet' as the default type unless the user specifies otherwise. For very high-temperature process heating (Heater outlet > ~250-300 C with a large duty, i.e. acting like a process furnace rather than a utility exchanger), the heat-exchanger correlation under-costs the equipment — see 'equipment_without_verified_turton_correlation' for the fired-heater case.

vessel.notes: Use 'horizontal' for horizontal flash drums / 3-phase separators and atmospheric horizontal storage tanks; use 'vertical' for vertical separators, distillation/absorption column shells, and reactor vessels (CSTR/PFR/conversion/equilibrium/Gibbs). For an atmospheric storage tank, set design pressure Pd = 0 barg (or slightly above for relief margin) so the vessel pressure factor formula correctly returns FP = 1.0.

tray.capacity_parameter: Tray active/cross-sectional area, m^2 (cost is PER TRAY — multiply by tray count and the quantity factor Fq, see formulas.8_tray_quantity_factor)
tray.notes: FBM for trays = the material factor directly (no B1/B2/FP needed). Bare module cost for the complete tray section = Cp0_one_tray * FM * Nt * Fq, where Nt is the number of trays from DWSIM's column results (or shortcut column N stages). Packed columns are not separately covered here — see 'equipment_without_verified_turton_correlation'.

agitator.notes: This is the in-vessel agitator/impeller (e.g. a CSTR's stirrer), NOT DWSIM's 'Mixer' logical stream-combiner object — see non_equipment_dwsim_objects. Add this cost ON TOP OF the vessel cost for any agitated reactor or mixing tank. Bare module factor is a flat 1.38 regardless of material in Turton's table.

dwsim_object_mapping.notes: Pick the pump 'type' field that matches the service; default to centrifugal if unspecified, since it covers the vast majority of process pumps.
Centrifugal is the default for continuous gas compression; use reciprocating for high-pressure-ratio, low-flow duties.

dwsim_object_mapping.size_variable_in_dwsim: Heat Added / Duty (kW) -> convert to area, see heat_exchanger.notes
dwsim_object_mapping.notes: Use 'fired_heater' approximate scaling instead (see equipment_without_verified_turton_correlation) if outlet temperature / duty indicates a process furnace rather than a utility exchanger.

dwsim_object_mapping.size_variable_in_dwsim: Heat Removed / Duty (kW) -> convert to area, see heat_exchanger.notes
Calculated heat transfer area (m2), or UA and LMTD to back into area
dwsim_object_mapping.notes: Map DWSIM's plate-and-frame HX to 'multiple_pipe' as the closest available correlation if no dedicated plate-HX correlation is added later.

Conceptual-estimate practice is to fold control/block valve cost into the piping & instrumentation allowance (commonly 20-40% of total bare module cost) rather than cost each valve individually. Cost individually only if doing a detailed estimate with vendor quotes.

Piping is conventionally estimated as a percentage of purchased equipment cost (commonly 20-30% for fluid processes) rather than via a K1/K2/K3 correlation, because cost depends on plant layout/routing, not just diameter and length. If you want a length-based estimate anyway, use a $/m-by-nominal-diameter table from a vendor or cost-data handbook.
Instrumentation item; fold into the instrumentation cost allowance (commonly 5-15% of total bare module cost) rather than costing individually.

dwsim_object_mapping.cost_type: horizontal or vertical (per your L/D orientation choice)
dwsim_object_mapping.size_variable_in_dwsim: Calculated vessel volume (m3) from flowsheet vapour/liquid flow + chosen residence time and L/D ratio
dwsim_object_mapping.notes: DWSIM does not size the physical vessel itself — you must size the vessel (diameter, length, volume) from the converged stream flows using a residence-time/settling-velocity method (e.g. Souders-Brown for vapour-liquid), then feed the resulting volume into the vessel correlation.

dwsim_object_mapping.size_variable_in_dwsim: Calculated vessel volume (m3) — size as above, with boot volume for the second liquid phase added to the main volume

dwsim_object_mapping.cost_type: vertical (typical for atmospheric storage) or horizontal
dwsim_object_mapping.size_variable_in_dwsim: Volume (m3) from tank dimensions; set design pressure ~0 barg

dwsim_object_mapping.cost_type: vertical vessel + sieve or valve trays
dwsim_object_mapping.size_variable_in_dwsim: Shell: volume from calculated diameter x (Nt x tray spacing + disengagement allowance). Trays: tray area from diameter, Nt = number of stages from the converged column (or Fenske-Underwood-Gilliland N for the shortcut column).
dwsim_object_mapping.notes: Condenser and reboiler are normally separate DWSIM Cooler/Heater (or dedicated HX) objects in the flowsheet — cost them under 'heat_exchanger', not as part of the column itself, to avoid double counting. A kettle reboiler should use the 'kettle_reboiler' heat-exchanger type.

dwsim_object_mapping.cost_type: vertical vessel + sieve or valve trays
dwsim_object_mapping.size_variable_in_dwsim: Same approach as Distillation Column above

dwsim_object_mapping.dwsim_object: Conversion Reactor / Equilibrium Reactor / Gibbs Reactor
dwsim_object_mapping.size_variable_in_dwsim: Volume (m3) from assumed/specified residence time x volumetric throughput (DWSIM does not size these reactors mechanically — you must assume a residence time appropriate to the reaction)
dwsim_object_mapping.notes: Add 'agitator' cost on top if the reactor is mechanically stirred (typical for liquid-phase CSTR-type duty); omit it for a reactor representing a fixed/packed bed or simple holding vessel.

dwsim_object_mapping.cost_type: vertical vessel + turbine or impeller agitator
dwsim_object_mapping.size_variable_in_dwsim: Vessel: Volume (m3) from specified residence time. Agitator: shaft power (kW) from an assumed power-per-unit-volume (commonly 0.5-3 kW/m3 for liquid blending/suspension duty — DWSIM does not calculate this directly).

If modelled as a single large vessel: Volume (m3) directly from the reactor specification. If modelled as a jacketed/multitubular reactor needing close temperature control: cost as a 'multiple_pipe' heat exchanger using the total tube-side area instead.
dwsim_object_mapping.notes: Choose based on what the PFR physically represents in your process, not the DWSIM model type.
This is a flow-combining logic block (a pipe tee in reality), not the agitator/impeller equipment category above. Negligible standalone capital cost.
Flow-splitting logic block; negligible standalone capital cost.

This is an idealised mass-balance shortcut (you specify split fractions directly) rather than a rate-based model of a real machine. It has no inherent cost correlation — decide what real equipment it is standing in for (e.g. a membrane unit, an absorber, a partial condenser) and cost THAT instead.

dwsim_object_mapping.size_variable_in_dwsim: Solids mass flow (kg/h) or gas/liquid volumetric flow depending on the real equipment represented
dwsim_object_mapping.notes: Like Component Separator, this is an idealised solids/liquid split. Identify the real solid-liquid (or solid-gas) separation device intended (cyclone, hydrocyclone, centrifuge, filter) and use the corresponding approximate correlation.

non_equipment_dwsim_objects: Logical blocks in general (controllers, balances, comments)

equipment_without_verified_turton_correl.size_parameter: Absorbed heat duty, kW (or MM Btu/hr)
equipment_without_verified_turton_correl.approach: Use a generic exponential scaling rule C2 = C1 * (S2/S1)^n with a known reference cost C1 at a reference duty S1 (from a vendor budget quote or a published cost-data handbook such as Woods' 'Rules of Thumb in Engineering Practice' or Peters, Timmerhaus & West), with a typical cost exponent n in the range 0.7-0.85 for fired heaters.
equipment_without_verified_turton_correl.confidence: low-to-medium — no specific K1/K2/K3 triplet from Turton's table is reproduced here; do not present this as a Turton-verified correlation in your tool's documentation.

equipment_without_verified_turton_correl.size_parameter: Inlet gas or slurry volumetric flow, m3/h
equipment_without_verified_turton_correl.approach: Generic exponential scaling rule, n typically 0.5-0.65; calibrate against a vendor quote where possible.

equipment_without_verified_turton_correl.size_parameter: Bowl/basket volume or solids throughput, depending on type
equipment_without_verified_turton_correl.approach: No generic correlation included; centrifuge cost is strongly type- and duty-dependent (decanter vs. basket vs. disc-stack) — recommend a vendor budget quote.

equipment_without_verified_turton_correl.equipment: Filter (continuous cake / rotary vacuum / pressure leaf)
equipment_without_verified_turton_correl.approach: Generic exponential scaling rule, n typically 0.55-0.65.

equipment_without_verified_turton_correl.equipment: Packed column internals (random or structured packing)
equipment_without_verified_turton_correl.size_parameter: Packed volume, m3, or packing surface area
equipment_without_verified_turton_correl.approach: Cost per m3 of packing from a vendor/handbook ($/m3 varies hugely by packing material and type — metal vs. plastic vs. ceramic, random vs. structured), multiplied by packed volume; not covered by the tray correlation above.
equipment_without_verified_turton_correl.confidence: not provided — tray correlation above is for trayed columns only.

non_equipment_dwsim_objects_note: These are calculation/logic constructs in the flowsheet, not physical plant items, and carry no direct equipment cost. Exclude them from the equipment list passed into the cost calculator.
equipment_without_verified_turton_correl: These items are intentionally left without fabricated K1/K2/K3 numbers, because no verified primary-source figure was available at compile time. Presenting an invented-looking precise correlation here would undermine the accuracy goal of this dataset more than leaving it as a flagged gap.
